description_long: This object is a large, ground-mounted solar energy installation featuring multiple rectangular solar panels arranged in a grid-like pattern. The panels are supported by a robust structural framework, likely made of metal, and are elevated above a base platform with various geometric features and levels. The surface of the panels is dark and grid-patterned, typical of photovoltaic cells, while the base includes a mix of flat and stepped surfaces, possibly for maintenance access or structural support. Several vertical and horizontal elements suggest cable management or housing for electrical components. The overall design is industrial and utilitarian, optimized for maximum solar exposure and durability in an urban environment. The installation appears to be intended for large-scale energy generation, such as in a solar farm or urban power station.
description: A large, ground-mounted solar energy installation with multiple grid-patterned panels on a structured base, featuring stepped and flat surfaces for support and access.
description_view_0: Front view showing the main array of solar panels elevated above a stepped and flat base, with visible support structures and geometric features.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the tilt of the solar panels and the depth of the supporting base, with more pronounced vertical supports visible.
description_view_2: Rear view displaying the back of the panels and the stepped base, with less visibility of the panel surfaces and more of the structural framework.
description_view_3: Opposite side view showing the angled arrangement of the panels and the varied elevation of the base, with support structures prominent.
attribute_panel_orientation: Panels are tilted at approximately 25-30 degrees, orientation appears fixed, azimuth direction not clearly specified.
attribute_mounting_structure_type: ground-mounted with a metal (likely aluminum or steel) frame and support columns.
attribute_array_configuration: Multiple rows of panels arranged in a staggered grid, approximately 3-4 large panels in total, spaced across the base.
